segments.text: The next 2 times 3.14.131, the Buddha said,
monks possessed of three qualities, a fighting man is worthy of a raja.
It's a royal possession, and it's reckoned and asset to a raja.
What are the three qualities?
Here in monks, a fighting man is a far shooter, a shooter like lightning,
and a piercing of huge objects, possess of these three qualities.
A fighting man worthy of a raja is a royal possession,
and it's reckoned and asset to a raja.
In light manner, monks possessed of three qualities,
a monk is worthy of respect of offerings and gifts,
or being saluted with claps, hands, uprights,
a few of merit unsaparse for the world.
What three? In this connection, a monk is a far shooter,
a shooter like lightning, a piercing of huge objects.
Now in what way is a monk a far shooter?
Here in whatsoever object be, past, future or present,
personal or external to self, be it gross or subtle,
mean or exalted, far or near, every object in short that he be holds.
He sees it as it really is by right inside us.
This is, this is not in mind, this is in mind not,
this is not in mind self.
Whatsoever feeling, be it past, future or present,
if he is, he sees it as it really is in light manner,
by right inside.
Whatsoever perception, whatsoever volition,
whatsoever consciousness he has, be it past, present or future, personal,
or external to self, be it gross or subtle, mean or exalted, far or near,
everything in short of which he is conscious.
He sees as it really is by right inside us.
This is not in mind, this is not in mind, this is not in mind self.
That is how a monk is a far shooter.
I'll just explain a bit here.
I'll stop for a while.
Here the Buddha is saying,
the Buddha is referring to five things.
He made this statement about this is not in mind, this is in mind not,
this is not in mind self.
He refers to five things.
The first one is form or body.
Second one is feelings, third is perception, then volition,
and the last one is consciousness.
These are called the five conducts, five aggregates.
And these five aggregates, the Buddha sees,
we always associate with the self, either we take it to be the self
or as belonging to the self or the self is in the five aggregates,
or the five aggregates is in the self.
For example, body.
If the body is tall, you say, I am tall,
if the body is short, you say, I am short,
if the body is sick, you say, I am sick.
So we always associate the body with the self, feelings also.
When we have happy feelings, we say, I'm happy.
When we have sad feelings, we say, I'm sad.
We find it very difficult.
To this, we'll see ourselves from these five things.
The third one is perception.
We have a perception of somebody as being likeable.
Somebody as being repulsive.
And then again, you say, this person is likeable to me,
or repulsive to me, et cetera.
And volition also, whenever volition is exercised,
we say, I want this, I don't want this.
And the last one is consciousness.
When we are conscious of sites, of sounds, of smells,
taste, touch, and thoughts, we say that we are conscious of this.
We see this, I see this, I hear this, that sound.
So these five things, five conducts, we always associate with the self.
And these five conducts can be broken down into two,
namely body and mind, the active mind.
So we always associate the body and the mind with the eye of the self.
And these statements, the Buddha, the first one is, this is not mind.
No, etang, mama.
Second one, this, and mind, not, not, is so, ahang asmeen.
This is not myself.
Now, me, eta, ah, these, these, these statements,
and I look carefully, and this is not mind, or the mind, or the mind.
This, and mind, not, this is not myself.
This implies, ah, one of two things, or two things, that,
this, because the, the things in the world, ah, basically also, ah,
can see body and ah, the mental part, mind, part.
And, and so whatever is in creation is not mind, it is not eye.
This is not myself.
That means, either, we can see there is no self, or whatever self, ah,
that we take ourselves to be, ah, the, the, if there is a self, ah,
is nothing to do with all that exists in the world, ah, but this is very hard to understand.
And, let's, we study the damma, and then we meditate, and then we look
carefully into our mind, see how the mind works.
And then, ah, you find, ah, everything that comes up in the mind,
it's like, ah, it's just like imagination, ah, ah, ah,
and then if you look carefully into the mind, or so, you find the, in a way that the body is
also in the mind, although, when our eyes are open, we see the body in this, as
do, it's very, ah, tangible, it's very solid, when we close our eyes and we meditate,
then we perceive the body in our mind, throughout the body is this, ah,
how we perceive the body, like, ah, perceptions, ah, like, ah, feelings,
whether the body is comfortable or comfortable or uncomfortable, ah, uncomfortable, ah,
pain, ah, that's pain in the body, ah, et cetera, all this, ah, in the mind,
then the Buddha continues, and how is the monk, a shooter, like, like,
I mean, here in the monk understands that as it really is, this is Dukka, this is
ill, this is the rising of Dukka, this is the ending of Dukka, this is the practice
leading to the ending of Dukka, thus is he a shooter, like, lightning, and how is the monk,
a piece of huge objects, here in a monk pierces through the huge mass of ignorance,
a delusion, that is how he is a piece of huge objects, thus monks possess
of these three qualities, a monk is worthy of offerings, a few of merit and surpass
for the world, so these three things are even monk hairs, ah, one, he sees at the body,
and the mind is not the self, then the second one, ah, he understands the four noble truths,
the third one, he pierces through, ah, the huge mass of ignorance or delusion,
and now he comes to the next sutra, which is three point, 14 point, one, three, four,
seven, monks, whether there is the appearance of a tatagata or there is not the appearance
of a tatagata, there is this established condition of Dhamma, this fixed law of Dhamma,
namely, all conditions are impermanent, sabisang para anicya, about this
tatagata is fully enlightened, he fully understands, so enlightened and understanding, he
declares, teaches and makes it plain, he shows it forth, he opens it up, explains and makes
it clear that all conditioners are impermanent, this stop here for a moment, here the Buddha
is saying, ah, whether the Buddha appears in the world or the Buddha does not appear in the world,
there is this established condition of Dhamma, this fixed law of Dhamma,
that means Dhamma is not something that was created by the Buddha, the Dhamma, the teachings of
the Buddha can also be known as reality or the truth about existence, ah, and it's just that
the Buddha has enlightened to the working of the existence, to the reality of existence,
and then he, having understood it, so he declares it and explains it and shows it forth,
the teachers, the first one that, the first characteristic of existence, and I said,
all conditioners are impermanent, this word, sabisang para anicya very often is translated as
all condition things are impermanent, but I'm one of those monks who prefer to translate
sanghara as conditioners, because the word for condition things are actually sanghata,
the word for the word sanghara is better translated as conditioners, something that condition
something else, and anything that condition something else is eventually also a condition thing
but then why did the Buddha say that all conditioners are impermanent, because in this existence
if the conditioners of something is impermanent then that thing is even more impermanent,
and this is given in one sutain majimani kaya number 146, where the Buddha said that,
for example that if two examples I think one is that take a tree, when the sun shines on the tree
creates a shadow, now the shadow is dependent on the tree, it's also dependent on there being the sunlight
so the conditioner of the shadow is the tree, the presence of the tree, and the presence of
sunlight, and these two conditioners are impermanent, because the sunlight is only there for a few hours,
and also the tree is also impermanent one day before, so because of that the shadow is even more
impermanent, so in the same way like a lamb is burning, so the radians of the light, given out
by the lamb burning, to understand first to understand as impermanent we look and see that the
conditioners of the brightness of the lamb depends on the weak, depends on the oil that is present
in the lamb, depends on the air being present, so if the weak is burned up or the all is used up
then the flame would not be there, so if you see that the conditioners of anything are impermanent,
even more impermanent would be that thing, and then the Buddha continues, monks,
whether there is the appearance of a tatagata or there is not the appearance of a tatagata,
there is this established condition of dhamma, this fixed law of dhamma, namely all
conditioners are duca, painful, about this tatagata is fully enlightened, if we understand,
et cetera, and then the Buddha continues and monks, whether there is the appearance of a tatagata,
or there is not the appearance of a tatagata, there is this established condition of dhamma,
this fixed law of dhamma, namely all things are not the cell, sabid dhamma anata, about this
tatagata is fully enlightened, if we understand it, so enlightenment and understanding he declares
and teaches it, makes it plain, he shows it for it, opens it up, explains and makes it clear
that all things are not the cell, so the first one I explained about all conditioners are impermanent,
and the second one because conditioners of anything are impermanent, so that thing is impermanent
and because of it being impermanent, it gives rise to duca, duca is sometimes translated as unsatisfactory,
sometimes as painful, sometimes as suffering, and we can understand why, is for example,
those that we love, our relatives, our family, because of everything in the world being impermanent,
there would be change and separation, like your children, when a young, you see them so cute,
and after some time they grow up and then they get married and they have to leave you,
and then as you grow old, then you have to leave all your beloved ones, and that causes pain,
duca, and also the things that we want, and we cannot get also it causes the pain,
the material things, and if we get it, sometimes we can't enjoy it long enough, so the body also gives
the pain, and the mind also gives the pain, that's why the Buddha said in short, the five
conducts that we cling to as the self associated with the self, it gives the pain, and then the third
thing is the Buddha said, all things are not self, all things are not self, and and and self,
he's explained, the acta is explained in Majimanikaya 148, as referring to something,
eternally abiding, not subject to conditions, and sometimes people translate at the self, as
the soul, something that is of this world, and eternally abiding is there is no such thing,
but like in the Hindu teachings they talk about soul also, but we have to understand them,
because those Hindu yogis who are well developed, the understand that the soul is not something
of this world, even though they say there is a soul, they say there is something that is out of this
world, something that the mind cannot, cannot perceive, come to another such a tree point 16.151,
the Buddha said, monks, there are these tree practices, what tree, practice of the practice of the
hodans and shulis, then of the self-torment and the middle path. Now one is the practice of the
hodans and shulis, here in monks, the certain one who does speaks and holds this view,
there is no fall in central desires, and he comes to utter ruin thereby, this practice
monks is called that of the hodans and shulis, this stop here for a while, here the Buddha is talking
about three, two extreme views, one is the person who enjoys central pleasures and who sees that
there is no nothing wrong in central desires, and so the Buddha said he comes to utter ruin,
now during the Buddha's days there were quite a lot of naked aesthetics, a man naked aesthetics as
well as women naked aesthetics, and because they were naked sometimes they look at each other
and then they are aroused and then they engage in sex, and they see that there's nothing wrong with it,
that it doesn't harm anybody else, but then the Buddha sees that this engaging in this central
harm, practice them, especially for a renunciation or bring one into the warful planes,
and then the Buddha continues, and of what sort is the practice of the self-torment,
here in a certain one goes naked, he does not follow convention, he licks his hands clean,
he will not come when asked, not stop when asked, food brought to him, he refuses special food,
he refuses an invitation to a meme, he refuses food straight from the pot, or straight from the
bone, or across the threshold of a door, across a stick, across a special vessel,
he refuses food when a couple are eating, or from a pregnant woman, or from a woman giving
some, or a woman lying with a man, he refuses food from a food distribution centre,
or where a dark stands by, or a flies asswarming, he eats no fish or flesh, drinks no liquor or
intoxicant, not even rice groan, he is a beggar from one house only, and eat a one mouthful,
or maybe he backs from two houses, eats two mouthfuls, up to seven houses, and eats seven mouthfuls
only, he exists on just one little source of food, or on just two, or more up to seven
source of food, he takes food only once a day, or once in two days, or three days,
even to the extent of eating once in 14 days, he feeds on only vegetables, or on millet,
or on grow rice, or on scraps of leather, or on water plants, or rice powder, or burns
come of rice, or flower, or all seeds, or on grass, or cow dung, or he just exists on
for his roots and on fruit that has fallen, he wears course-hampened clothes, or cloth,
of different fibers, or discarded cups, cloths, cloth, or rags from a rubbish heap, or tree-bap,
fiber, or antelope, skins, or strips of antelope, a skin, or clothes made of kusa grass,
made of wood-cheavings, blankets made of human hair, made of horse hair, made of owls-wings,
he is a plucker of the hair and beard, and given to this practice, he remains standing and refuses
to see, he squats down and keeps a squatting posture, he is a bed of thornsman, he makes his bed
on spikes, leaves, given to the practice of going down to the water to be it, even a third time in
the evening also, thus in diverse ways, he lives, given to these practices which torment the body,
this mounds is called the practice of self-domain, and of what's for mounds is the middle part,
here in a monk lives contemplating body in the body, ardent, self-possessed, and mindful,
having put away covetousness and dejection for the world, similarly he contemplates feelings
in the feelings, mind in the mind, the mind, the dama, ardent, self-possessed, and mindful,
having put away covetousness and dejection for the world. This is called the middle
part, these are the three practices. So you see, the second type of person is one who commands himself,
he does a lot of unbeneficial aesthetic practices, some of them you can understand, for example,
he refuses food when a dog is standing by, or when flies as warming, because he thinks he's
practicing compassion. So if the of, for example, when a woman is giving milk to her baby, yeah,
and the baby is sucking a milk, and if she offers food, and this person will think,
oh, because if I take this food now, there'll be not enough food, not enough milk for the child,
or the dog won't get the food, or the flies won't get the food, so he refuses to take the
food. And the others are not so reasonable, he stops himself and all that, and this is unbeneficial
aesthetic practices. But the Buddha said the middle part, it's a part that if we practice,
we will bring us wisdom, and because it gives us out of some sort of out of the round of
three birds. These are the sati-patana, contemplating the four objects of sati-patana, the body,
the feelings, mind, and the dama. And with that now, we come to the end of the first book.
